title: Long-Context & Prompt Caching — Window Management, Caching & Eviction
description: At Staff level, context engineering is about systematically managing the input context budget: understanding context window mechanics, lost-in-the-middle effects, KV cache management, prompt caching strategies, and context-aware routing to maximize quality within memory constraints.

sections.title: 1. Context Window Mechanics & Budgeting
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Transformer attention is O(N²) in compute and O(N × d) in memory per layer. A 128K context at 70B parameters consumes ~640 GB of HBM for the KV cache alone — you can't fit one request on an A100 (80 GB). Budget your context like memory on an embedded system: reserve 10% for system prompt, 20% for output tokens (autoregressive generation), and 70% for user content.

bar_chart Concrete Numbers: LLaMA 2 70B, FP16, batch=1, 100K context: KV cache = 2 × 80 layers × 100K tokens × 8192 d_model × 2 bytes = ~262 GB. More precisely: KV cache per token = 2 × n_layers × d_model × precision. For LLaMA 2 (80 layers, 8192 dim, FP16): 2 × 80 × 8192 × 2 = 2.6 MB per token — at 128K tokens that's 333 GB. GPT-4 128K uses 8× tensor parallelism across 8 A100s just to hold one context.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Estimate KV cache size before deploying: (n_layers × d_model × 2 × precision_bytes) bytes per token. Set per-request token budgets in your router (e.g. 32K for fast path, 128K for slow path). Reject or route to RAG when budget exceeded. Monitor OOM rates in production to tune limits.

search Interview Angle: "Our 70B model with 128K context keeps OOMing. Walk me through how you'd diagnose and fix it." Expected answer: compute the KV cache memory, identify the bottleneck (HBM capacity), propose solutions: decrease batch size, use sliding window, switch to MQA/GQA, quantize KV cache to FP8, or deploy with tensor parallelism across more GPUs.
sections.codeBlock:
def estimate_kv_cache_memory(n_layers: int, d_model: int, context_len: int, precision_bytes: int = 2) -> dict:
    """Estimate KV cache memory per request in GB."""
    kv_per_token = 2 * n_layers * d_model * precision_bytes
    total_kv_cache = kv_per_token * context_len
    # ponytail: LLaMA 2 70B, 128K context, FP16
    return {
        "kv_cache_bytes_per_token": kv_per_token,
        "kv_cache_mb_per_token": kv_per_token / (1024**2),
        "total_kv_cache_gb": total_kv_cache / (1024**3),
        "can_fit_on_a100_80gb": total_kv_cache < 80e9,
    }


def budget_context(system_pct: float = 0.1, output_pct: float = 0.2, max_tokens: int = 128000) -> dict:
    content_pct = 1.0 - system_pct - output_pct
    # ponytail: reserves 30% for system+output, 70% for actual content
    return {
        "system_budget": int(max_tokens * system_pct),
        "output_budget": int(max_tokens * output_pct),
        "content_budget": int(max_tokens * content_pct),
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    mem = estimate_kv_cache_memory(80, 8192, 128000)
    print(mem)
    budget = budget_context()
    print(f"Content budget: {budget['content_budget']} tokens")

sections.title: 2. Lost in the Middle & Positional Bias
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLMs exhibit a U-shaped accuracy curve: they perform best on information at the very start (primacy) and very end (recency) of the context, with a significant accuracy drop (~20-40%) for information in the middle. This is not a bug — it's a consequence of the softmax attention distribution and positional encoding decay. Models learn to weight recent and initial tokens more heavily.

bar_chart Concrete Numbers: Liu et al. (2023) showed that for 10-key retrieval tasks, accuracy at position 0 (first key) is ~85%, drops to ~45% at position 5000 (middle), and recovers to ~75% at position 10000 (last). Multi-document QA shows a similar 35-40 point drop between head/tail and middle documents. Even GPT-4-Turbo shows a 15-20 point degradation in the middle third of a 128K window. Mixture-of-depth architectures partially mitigate this by re-weighting attention across layers.

track_changes Practical Workflow: (1) Put critical instructions in the system prompt (start of context). (2) Put few-shot examples at the very end, just before the query. (3) For RAG, structure results as ranked list with most relevant at start and end; place medium-relevance results in the middle. (4) Repeat critical instructions at both beginning and end. (5) Use structured formats (XML/JSON) — models attend better to delimited sections than free text.

search Interview Angle: "Your RAG pipeline retrieves 20 documents. The model keeps ignoring document #10 even though it's the most relevant. Why and how do you fix it?" Expected answer: lost-in-the-middle effect — restructure the context to place critical info at start/end, truncate to fewer but higher-quality docs, or use structured formats like XML tags to guide attention.
sections.codeBlock:
import random
from typing import List, Any


def lost_in_the_middle_mitigation(docs: List[Any], top_k: int = 10) -> List[Any]:
    """Reorder docs to place highest-relevance at start and end.
    
    Args:
        docs: list of (relevance_score, content) tuples
        top_k: number of docs to include
    """
    sorted_docs = sorted(docs, key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)[:top_k]
    # ponytail: simplest mitigation — bookend best docs
    # Upgrade path: use a learned re-ranker for graded positioning
    best = sorted_docs[:top_k // 4]
    middle = sorted_docs[top_k // 4: 3 * top_k // 4]
    rest = sorted_docs[3 * top_k // 4: top_k]
    reordered = best + middle + rest
    return reordered


def build_context_with_bookends(system_prompt: str, query: str, docs: List[Any], final_instruction: str = "Answer concisely using the provided documents.") -> str:
    reordered = lost_in_the_middle_mitigation(docs)
    doc_text = "\n\n".join(f"<doc id={i}>{c}</doc>" for i, (_, c) in enumerate(reordered))
    context = f"{system_prompt}\n\n{doc_text}\n\n{query}\n\n{final_instruction}"
    return context


if __name__ == "__main__":
    docs = [(random.random(), f"doc_{i}") for i in range(20)]
    ctx = build_context_with_bookends("System: answer from docs.", "Q: what is X?", docs)
    print(f"Context length: {len(ctx)} chars")

sections.title: 3. Prompt Caching Strategies
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The KV cache for a system prompt + few-shot examples is identical across many requests. Instead of recomputing it every time (O(N²) attention per request), cache the KV tensors for the prompt prefix. Hash-based dedup lets you reuse cached prefixes with near-zero latency. This is the single biggest optimization for latency-sensitive LLM applications.

bar_chart Concrete Numbers: A 2K-token system prompt on LLaMA 2 70B takes ~400 ms to prefill on an A100. Caching the prefix KV reduces time-to-first-token (TTFT) to ~5 ms for the cached portion — an 80× improvement. For a chatbot with a 4K system prompt serving 1000 QPS, caching reduces prefill compute by ~80%, saving ~$0.50 per million tokens in compute cost. Anthropic's prompt caching reduces latency by up to 85% for long system prompts. OpenAI's automatic prompt caching (since 2024-09) gives a 50% discount on cached input tokens.

track_changes Practical Workflow: (1) Identify the static prefix in every request (system prompt, tool definitions, few-shot examples). (2) Hash the prefix and store the KV cache in an LRU cache mapped by this hash. (3) On each request, check if the cached prefix matches; if yes, compute attention only on the suffix. (4) Monitor cache hit rate — below 60% means your prefix varies too much. (5) Design prompt engineering to maximize the static prefix: put variable content at the end of the context, not the beginning.

search Interview Angle: "We want to serve a model with a 10K-token system prompt at 500 QPS on 4 A100s. The TTFT is 2 seconds — unacceptably slow. How do you reduce it?" Expected answer: prompt caching — precompute KV for the static system prompt, store in GPU HBM (or CPU RAM with async transfer for large caches), reuse across requests. Estimate: 10K tokens at 2.6 MB/token = 26 GB saved per request, reducing prefill from O(10K²) to O(variable²).

sections.title: 4. Context Eviction & Sliding Window
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: When the conversation or document exceeds the context window, you must decide what to evict. Three strategies: (1) summarization — compress old turns into a summary token budget, (2) sliding window — keep only the last N tokens (e.g. 4K) and discard the rest, (3) chunk-and-process — split input into overlapping chunks and process independently, then aggregate. Each has latency-cost-quality tradeoffs that must be tuned per use case.

bar_chart Concrete Numbers: Sliding window with window=4096 on Mistral 7B uses KV cache = 2 × 32 × 4096 × 4096 × 2 = 2.1 GB vs. full 32K = 17 GB — an 8× memory savings. Summarization adds ~500 ms latency per summarization step using a cheap model (GPT-4o-mini) but preserves context across arbitrarily long conversations. Chunking with overlap=200 on a 100K document costs 4× the API calls but enables parallel processing with ~2× total wall time for 4 chunks in parallel.

track_changes Practical Workflow: (1) Set a hard token limit per request. (2) When exceeding, apply a tiered eviction policy: first drop older turns beyond a rolling window, then summarize if the window still exceeds budget, then truncate. (3) Use a secondary model (cheap, fast) for summarization rather than the primary model. (4) For long documents, use chunking with overlap and generate intermediate answers per chunk, then final synthesis.

search Interview Angle: "We have a multi-turn chatbot that routinely exceeds 32K tokens. Users complain the model forgets what they said 10 turns ago. How do you balance memory and quality?" Expected answer: implement a tiered eviction policy — keep a sliding window of 8K raw tokens, summarize older turns into a 2K rolling summary, place the summary at the start of context. Monitor recall of old-turn facts to tune the window size vs. summary length tradeoff.

sections.title: 5. Context-Aware Routing
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Not every query needs a 128K context model. Route short queries (few-shot classification, extraction) to a small, fast model with a small context window. Route long-context queries (document analysis, multi-turn conversation) to a model with a larger window. This is cost-effective inference: ~80% of production queries can be served by a 7B model with a 4K context, dramatically reducing infrastructure costs.

bar_chart Concrete Numbers: GPT-4o-mini (8K context) costs $0.15/M input tokens vs. GPT-4o (128K context) at $2.50/M — a 16.7× cost difference. If 80% of your 10M daily queries fit in 4K context, routing saves ~$18,000/day in API costs. Self-hosted: a 7B model with 4K context on a single L4 GPU costs $0.50/hr vs. a 70B model on 4× A100s at $16/hr — a 32× infrastructure cost reduction.

track_changes Practical Workflow: (1) Examine request metadata: token count, required tools/plugins, latency SLA. (2) Define routing rules: context ≤ 4K → fast model (7B), 4K < context ≤ 32K → medium model (34B), 32K < context ≤ 128K → large model (70B). (3) Use a lightweight classifier or simple heuristics to determine intent complexity beyond context size. (4) Monitor routing decisions — if too many requests go to the large model, tune thresholds or optimize prompt engineering to reduce context size.

search Interview Angle: "Our serving costs are $100K/month. Your task is to reduce them by 50% without degrading response quality. What do you do?" Expected answer: context-aware routing — analyze the context length distribution, route 80% of queries to a smaller model, implement prompt caching for the rest, and monitor quality metrics per model to ensure no degradation.

sections.title: 6. Production Metrics for Context Management
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: You can't optimize what you don't measure. Context engineering requires a dedicated dashboard tracking: effective context utilization (how much of the budget is actually used?), cache efficiency (what fraction of prefill compute is saved?), eviction patterns (how often and why are contexts dropped?), and cost breakdown by strategy. These metrics inform every tuning decision for window size, caching policies, and routing thresholds.

bar_chart Concrete Numbers: Typical production patterns: 60-70% cache hit rate for chatbot system prompts, 85%+ for code completion prefixes. Context utilization averages 45-55% of budget for RAG pipelines. Eviction rates above 20% indicate the window is too small. Cost per query: without caching = $0.012, with caching = $0.003 (a 75% reduction). Per-token cost: cached tokens cost ~50% less on OpenAI or effectively $0 for self-hosted prefix caching.

track_changes Practical Workflow: (1) Log every request with: total_tokens, cached_prefix_tokens, eviction_count, model_routed_to, latency. (2) Build a dashboard (Grafana/Datadog) with: context utilization histogram, cache hit rate over time, eviction rate by conversation length, cost per request by model tier. (3) Set alert thresholds: cache hit rate < 50% → review prompt design, eviction rate > 15% → increase window or improve summarization, context utilization < 30% → encourage users to be more specific.

search Interview Angle: "Your team launched a chatbot with prompt caching. Users complain it's not faster. What metrics do you look at first?" Expected answer: check cache hit rate (likely low if every user prompt is unique — system prompt has high variability), check effective context utilization (users sending long contexts but model only uses small portion), and compare prefill latency vs. decode latency. Follow-up: redesign prompts to maximize static prefix, move variable content to the end.
sections.codeBlock:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List


@dataclass
class ContextMetrics:
    total_requests: int = 0
    cached_requests: int = 0
    total_tokens: int = 0
    cached_tokens: int = 0
    evictions: int = 0
    latencies_ms: List[float] = field(default_factory=list)
    costs: List[float] = field(default_factory=list)

    def record(self, tokens: int, cached: int, evicted: bool, latency_ms: float, cost: float):
        self.total_requests += 1
        if cached > 0:
            self.cached_requests += 1
        self.total_tokens += tokens
        self.cached_tokens += cached
        self.evictions += 1 if evicted else 0
        self.latencies_ms.append(latency_ms)
        self.costs.append(cost)

    def report(self) -> dict:
        sorted_lat = sorted(self.latencies_ms)
        return {
            "cache_hit_rate": self.cached_requests / max(self.total_requests, 1),
            "tokens_cached_ratio": self.cached_tokens / max(self.total_tokens, 1),
            "eviction_rate": self.evictions / max(self.total_requests, 1),
            "p50_latency_ms": sorted_lat[len(sorted_lat) // 2] if sorted_lat else 0,
            "avg_cost_per_request": sum(self.costs) / max(len(self.costs), 1),
            "total_cost": sum(self.costs),
        }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    metrics = ContextMetrics()
    # ponytail: simulate 10000 requests with 65% cache hit rate
    import random
    for _ in range(10000):
        tokens = random.randint(500, 8000)
        cached = int(tokens * 0.7) if random.random() < 0.65 else 0
        evicted = random.random() < 0.08
        latency = 150 + random.gauss(0, 30) if cached else 800 + random.gauss(0, 200)
        cost = (tokens / 1_000_000) * (0.05 if cached else 0.15)
        metrics.record(tokens, cached, evicted, latency, cost)
    for k, v in metrics.report().items():
        print(f"{k}: {v:.4f}")

details: Context engineering is the discipline of managing an LLM's limited context window as a system resource. At Staff level, you are responsible for the inference infrastructure that serves millions of requests: you must decide what fits in the window, what gets cached, what gets evicted, and which model serves each request. The canonical references are Liu et al. (2023) Lost in the Middle for positional bias analysis and Vaswani et al. (2017) for attention mechanics. Modern systems like Anthropic's prompt caching, OpenAI's automatic prompt caching, and Mistral's sliding window attention implement these ideas in production. The key insight is that context is not free — O(N²) attention and O(N × d × L) KV cache memory mean context management directly impacts latency, throughput, and cost at scale. This file covers the six pillars of context engineering: window mechanics, positional bias mitigation, prompt caching, eviction strategies, context-aware routing, and production observability.
